5 min read + 17 video
A Customer Onboarding Series is a hybrid of the AoE Trust and
Customer Onboarding Series
(COS)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 8 --

Revenue Engines. It’s part of the Revenue Engine because a COS is
triggered after a purchase. And it’s part of the Trust Engine
because a COS establishes a relationship with new customers and
expands relationships with existing customers.
Let’s start with the mechanics.
First, a COS is triggered immediately after someone makes a
purchase. This �rst email is an opportunity to thank a customer
and tell him/her what to expect next. If other emails are sent
automatically (for example, a receipt, login information, etc.),
reference those in the �rst email.
It’s also a good idea to include information about contacting
customer support in this �rst email. (“If you have any questions,
reply to this email…”)
Second, the number and frequency of emails will depend entirely
on your speci�c situation. For example, you may decide to send
seven emails daily immediately after purchase, followed by �ve
weekly check-ins and a “congratulations, you made it” email at
some predetermined interval.
There is no ‘right’ answer for everyone because every situation is
different.
Third, a Customer Onboarding Series is not an excuse to sell
something else immediately after a purchase. We’ve all
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 8 --

experienced this:
“Hey friend, we noticed you bought X,Y,Z…come back this week and
save 20% with code SAVE20 …”
There are three common scenarios where you’ll use a Customer
Onboarding Series.
Scenario #1 — a new prospect (who is not on your email list)
makes a purchase. That person has not been exposed to a
Relationship Building Series.
Scenario #2 — an existing prospect (not a paying customer)
makes their �rst purchase. That person has been exposed to a
Relationship Building Series (at a minimum) and likely other
email communication from you.
Scenario #3 — an existing customer makes a repeat purchase.
That person has been exposed to a Relationship Building Series,
other email communication from you, and (almost certainly) a
Customer Onboarding Series for other purchases.
In each of these scenarios, the COS has a lot of work to do.
In Scenario #1, a COS tells the brand-new customer what it’s like
to be part of your world. S/he likely was exposed to other
communication from you (landing pages, products pages,
worldbuilding content, etc.) — but the COS is where the inbox
relationship between you and the customer begins.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 8 --

In Scenario #2, a COS expands on a relationship that already
exists. However, because this is the �rst purchase, the stakes are
high and the customer is learning what to expect when s/he takes
a leap of faith and makes a purchase.
In Scenario #3, a COS further reinforces (or, if we’re not careful,
undermines) an existing customer relationship. Scenario #3 is a
spectrum — a repeat customer could be making a second
purchase or many more.
For example, someone who enters our world by purchasing the
Art of Email could become a repeat customer by purchasing
Sphere of In�uence, The Traf�c Engine, or the Durable Business.
Or someone could own SOI, TTE, and TDB, and the Art of Email is
their fourth purchase!
We mention the intricacies of all of these scenarios not to
overwhelm you with dozens of options. While you certainly could
customize a COS for different audience segments, we don’t think
that’s necessary.
Instead, we think a Customer Onboarding Series, done well, can
work well for every scenario as long as we’re focused on the needs
of each audience.
Those needs are transparency, trust, value, and congruence. Let’s
look at each individually.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 8 --

Transparency is a catch-all term for telling our customers what
to expect, when to expect it, and what to do if something goes
wrong.
Trust emerges when we continue to reaf�rm that the customer
made a good decision through our actions and the quality of our
communications.
Trust is built (slowly) over time, and it can be broken easily and
quickly. Doing what we say we’ll do (or being open and honest
when we don’t) is an important place to start.
Sharing value is at the core of a great Customer Onboarding
Series. Broadly speaking, that can include drawing attention to
value in the product/service itself and/or creating additional value
in parallel with the product/service.
There is no right answer. Whatever makes sense for your speci�c
situation should inform your decisions.
When sharing value in a COS, be careful not to overwhelm your
customers, especially if you’re sending onboarding emails
frequently.
Daily emails, for example, should draw attention to 1-3 things,
with 1-3 action items (maximum). But, again, your speci�c
situation will dictate what’s best for your customers.
What matters most is that your emails are helping your
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 8 --

customers get the best experience possible from their relationship
with you.
Congruence is a little harder to explain. It’s easiest to notice
when it goes wrong. For example, our audiences will notice if we
are over the top in our marketing and sales emails and
underperform with our post-sale communication.
This is an all-too-common mistake. Marketing and selling are
over-emphasized, but delivering on the sale is an afterthought.
The alternative is to ensure that the customer experience is as
good, or better, than the experience of becoming a customer. We do
not want our customers to feel like we’ve forgotten them once
money has changed hands.
Here are a few questions to think about when creating a
Customer Onboarding Series.
What functional/logistical things happen that the customer needs
to know? For example, receipts, credentials, shipping information,
email frequency, and how to get help if something goes wrong.
How can you demonstrate empathy and authority in your COS?
What feelings is your new customer likely experiencing? How
can you help amplify the positive feelings and mitigate the
negative feelings?
For example, if new customers might feel overwhelmed, create a
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 8 --

road map or quick start guide.
Where should a customer start, and what should s/he expect?
What can you draw attention to that’s interesting and informative
during the customers’ journey? Is there anything you can expand
upon with backstories, additional details, anecdotes, or story
vignettes that add interest to the material?
Are there opportunities to include ‘Easter Eggs’ in your COS
(unexpected, over-the-top value, threads to pull that expand on
what you’ve created, etc.).
These questions only scratch the surface of what’s possible.
A helpful way to think about a Customer Onboarding Series is as a
reward for those people who have taken a big step toward you and
become customers. That’s a signi�cant turning point — and at the
core of all of the work we’re doing in the Art of Email.
When it happens, celebrate it and make your customers feel
special — this is an opportunity to delight them!
Please ask questions and leave comments below.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com
